Fast responses use paracrine signals; slow responses use endocrine hormones
Fast responses are driven by endocrine signaling; slow responses by contact-dependent signaling
questions.explain: Fast responses (seconds to minutes) alter pre-existing protein function directly — no gene expression or new synthesis needed, just activation of dormant machinery (e.g., opening ion channels or activating existing enzymes). Slow responses (minutes to hours) require the signal to alter transcription so new proteins can be synthesized. Options A and B reverse the relationship.

questions.q: Which of the following is the primary function of YAP and TAZ in the HIPPO pathway?
options: To phosphorylate DCH S1/2 and FAT4 receptors to activate the kinase cascade
To sequester 14-3-3 proteins in the cytoplasm, preventing their nuclear entry
To act as transcriptional co-activators in the nucleus, driving expression of pro-growth genes
To hydrolyze GTP on Ras-like proteins, terminating the proliferation signal
To form ion channels that allow Ca²⁺ entry and trigger downstream kinase activation
questions.explain: YAP and TAZ are transcriptional co-activators; when unphosphorylated they enter the nucleus and drive expression of pro-growth transcription factors, promoting cell proliferation. 14-3-3 proteins sequester phosphorylated YAP/TAZ in the cytoplasm (not the other way around), and YAP/TAZ have no GTPase or ion channel function.

questions.q: At high cell density, how does the HIPPO pathway prevent cell proliferation?
options: Cell-cell contacts inactivate DCH S1/2 and FAT4, allowing YAP/TAZ to enter the nucleus freely
Cell-cell contacts activate DCH S1/2 and FAT4, triggering a kinase cascade that phosphorylates YAP/TAZ, which are then retained in the cytoplasm by 14-3-3 proteins
High density directly inhibits transcription factor binding to pro-growth gene promoters without requiring kinase activity
YAP/TAZ phosphorylation at high density promotes their nuclear import and activation of apoptotic genes
High cell density saturates extracellular growth factor receptors, indirectly activating HIPPO by depleting available ligand
questions.explain: At high cell density, membrane-bound regulators DCH S1/2 and FAT4 are engaged by cell-cell contacts, activating a downstream kinase cascade that phosphorylates YAP/TAZ. Phosphorylated YAP/TAZ are recognized and sequestered in the cytoplasm by 14-3-3 proteins, preventing nuclear entry and blocking pro-growth gene expression — implementing contact inhibition.

questions.q: Which of the following statements about the mouse liver HIPPO experiment (Dong et al., Cell, 2007) is NOT correct?
options: Inducible expression of activated YAP was triggered using doxycycline
Liver/body weight ratio increased from approximately 5% to approximately 17% within two weeks
The liver overgrowth was reversible upon turning off the YAP transgene
Tumors developed within one week of transgene induction, demonstrating rapid oncogenesis
At eight weeks of continuous YAP activity, tumors developed in the liver
questions.explain: Tumors appeared at eight weeks, not within one week — the overgrowth over the first two weeks was reversible hyperplasia, not immediate tumor formation. Statements A, B, C, and E accurately describe the experimental data from Dong et al. The one-week claim in option D is a fabricated timeline that contradicts the published findings.

questions.q: A loss-of-function mutation in HIPPO pathway kinases (that normally phosphorylate YAP/TAZ) would be predicted to cause which outcome?
options: Constitutive YAP/TAZ inactivation and arrest of cell proliferation in all tissues
Constitutive nuclear localization of unphosphorylated YAP/TAZ and excessive cell proliferation or organ overgrowth
Increased 14-3-3-mediated cytoplasmic sequestration of YAP/TAZ, reducing pro-growth signaling
Enhanced activation of DCH S1/2 and FAT4 leading to accelerated contact inhibition
Selective apoptosis of high-density cell populations due to failure of the survival signal
questions.explain: If HIPPO kinases cannot phosphorylate YAP/TAZ, then YAP/TAZ remain unphosphorylated regardless of cell density and constitutively enter the nucleus to drive pro-growth gene expression — mimicking the low-density state even in crowded tissue. This leads to uncontrolled proliferation and organ overgrowth, consistent with the fruit fly wing disc data showing massive overgrowth when HIPPO signaling is disrupted.

questions.q: Which structural feature correctly describes G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs)?
options: Single-pass transmembrane protein with an intracellular kinase domain
Double-pass transmembrane protein with two membrane-spanning helices
Seven-pass transmembrane protein with seven membrane-spanning helices
Peripheral membrane protein anchored by a GPI lipid tail
Soluble nuclear receptor that binds lipid-soluble ligands in the cytoplasm
questions.explain: GPCRs are defined by their seven-pass (heptahelical) transmembrane topology. The most common error is confusing GPCRs with RTKs, which are single-pass transmembrane proteins; single-pass is the description of enzyme-coupled receptors, not GPCRs.

questions.q: Approximately what fraction of currently used drugs exert their effects through GPCRs?
options: ~5% of currently used drugs
~10% of currently used drugs
~20% of currently used drugs
~30% of currently used drugs
~50% of currently used drugs
questions.explain: Approximately 30% of all drugs in clinical use act through GPCRs, reflecting the enormous therapeutic importance of this receptor family. The ~400 olfactory receptor figure is a different statistic about the GPCR family size, not about drug targets.

questions.q: When a ligand binds an active GPCR and GDP dissociates from the Gα subunit, what event immediately follows?
options: Gα is phosphorylated by a receptor-associated kinase and becomes active
GTP binds Gα, causing Gα-GTP to dissociate from Gβγ
Gβγ hydrolyzes GTP to GDP and reassociates with Gα
The receptor internalizes and Gα is degraded by the proteasome
IP₃ is released from the membrane and diffuses to the ER
questions.explain: GDP dissociation creates an empty nucleotide-binding site on Gα; GTP (which is present at high concentrations in the cytosol) rapidly fills this site, producing Gα-GTP, which then dissociates from Gβγ. GTP hydrolysis (not GTP binding) is the inactivating step that comes later.

questions.q: What molecular event terminates the activity of Gα-GTP signaling?
options: A cytosolic phosphatase removes the phosphate from Gα
Gβγ binds back to Gα and blocks its effector-binding surface
The intrinsic GTPase activity of Gα hydrolyzes GTP to GDP
The ligand dissociates from the GPCR and allows Gα to re-associate
Ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation of Gα-GTP
questions.explain: The duration of Gα signaling is determined by the intrinsic GTPase (GTP-hydrolyzing) activity built into the Gα subunit itself. Once GTP is hydrolyzed to GDP, Gα returns to its inactive conformation and reassociates with Gβγ. Phosphatases and protein degradation are not the primary mechanism.

questions.q: After a GPCR ligand triggers G protein dissociation, which statement correctly describes the activity of the resulting subunits?
options: Only Gα-GTP is active; Gβγ remains inactive until Gα reassociates
Only Gβγ is active; Gα-GTP is sequestered by a chaperone protein
Both Gα-GTP and free Gβγ are active and each can activate distinct effectors
Neither subunit is active until the receptor is phosphorylated by GRK
Gα-GTP and Gβγ form a new heterotrimer with a different Gα isoform
questions.explain: Both Gα-GTP and free Gβγ are active effector-activating units after dissociation. For example, Gβγ directly opens K⁺ channels in heart pacemaker cells. Students frequently attribute all downstream activity only to Gα, missing the important role of Gβγ.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a direct effector activated by heterotrimeric G proteins downstream of a GPCR?
options: Adenylyl cyclase, producing cAMP from ATP
Phospholipase C, cleaving PIP₂ into IP₃ and DAG
Ion channels, such as K⁺ channels in cardiac pacemaker cells
Receptor tyrosine kinases, triggering phosphotyrosine docking
None of the above — all four are direct G-protein effectors
questions.explain: The three canonical G-protein effectors are adenylyl cyclase, phospholipase C, and ion channels. RTKs are a completely separate class of cell-surface receptors activated by growth factors, not by GPCR-coupled G proteins.

questions.q: In heart pacemaker cells, which subunit of the G protein directly opens K⁺ channels to slow heart rate after acetylcholine binds its muscarinic GPCR?
options: Gα-GDP, the inactive form of the alpha subunit
Gα-GTP, the active form of the alpha subunit
Gβγ, the free beta-gamma complex after G protein dissociation
The GPCR itself, which changes conformation to gate the channel
cAMP, generated by adenylyl cyclase after G protein activation
questions.explain: In heart pacemaker cells, the free Gβγ complex directly opens K⁺ channels, causing K⁺ efflux, hyperpolarization, and slowed heart rate. This is a key example of Gβγ-mediated effector activation, not Gα-mediated, which is a common source of confusion.

questions.q: Adrenaline binds its adrenergic GPCR, activates the Gs protein, and ultimately increases glycogen breakdown in muscle. Which intracellular relay correctly describes this pathway?
options: Gs → phospholipase C → IP₃ → Ca²⁺ → PKC → glycogen breakdown
Gs → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA → glycogen breakdown
Gs → PI3-kinase → PIP₃ → Akt → glycogen breakdown
Gs → Ras-GEF → Ras-GTP → MAPK → glycogen breakdown
Gs → guanylyl cyclase → cGMP → PKG → glycogen breakdown
questions.explain: The Gs/adenylyl cyclase/cAMP/PKA pathway is the canonical route through which adrenaline stimulates glycogen breakdown. The PLC/PKC and PI3K/Akt pathways are distinct and not involved in this adrenaline response.

questions.q: Which second messenger directly activates Protein Kinase A (PKA)?
options: Diacylglycerol (DAG) produced by PLC
Inositol trisphosphate (IP₃) released into the cytosol
Cyclic AMP (cAMP) synthesized by adenylyl cyclase
Calcium ions released from the endoplasmic reticulum
Phosphatidylinositol trisphosphate (PIP₃) in the inner leaflet
questions.explain: cAMP binds to the regulatory subunits of PKA, releasing the catalytic subunits and activating them. DAG and Ca²⁺ together activate PKC, not PKA — confusing these two pathways and their respective kinases is one of the most common exam errors.

questions.q: Phospholipase C (PLC) is activated by a G protein. Which pair of molecules does it generate by cleaving PIP₂?
options: cAMP and cGMP, two cyclic nucleotide second messengers
IP₃ and diacylglycerol (DAG), two distinct second messengers
PIP₃ and PDK1, a lipid second messenger and a kinase
GDP and GTP, exchanged on the Gα subunit
Arachidonic acid and lysophospholipid, prostaglandin precursors
questions.explain: PLC cleaves the membrane phospholipid PIP₂ into IP₃ (water-soluble, diffuses to ER) and DAG (lipid-soluble, stays in membrane). Both products are second messengers. Students often name only one — the exam expects both.

questions.q: After PLC cleaves PIP₂, IP₃ and DAG are generated. What is the primary initial source of the Ca²⁺ that is released into the cytosol?
options: Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels in the plasma membrane open in response to IP₃
IP₃ opens Ca²⁺ channels on the endoplasmic reticulum membrane
DAG chelates extracellular Ca²⁺ and transports it across the plasma membrane
Mitochondria release stored Ca²⁺ in response to elevated DAG
Adenylyl cyclase-generated cAMP opens ER Ca²⁺ channels indirectly
questions.explain: IP₃ (inositol trisphosphate) is water-soluble and diffuses to the ER membrane, where it binds and opens IP₃-gated Ca²⁺ channels, releasing Ca²⁺ from the ER lumen into the cytosol. The ER Ca²⁺ concentration (~1 mM) is far higher than resting cytosolic levels (~10⁻⁴ mM), driving rapid release.

questions.q: A sperm fertilizes an egg and a Ca²⁺ wave sweeps across the egg. Which signaling molecules are responsible for this Ca²⁺ release?
options: cAMP activates PKA, which phosphorylates ER Ca²⁺ pumps to open them
IP₃ opens Ca²⁺ channels on the ER, releasing stored Ca²⁺ into the cytosol
PIP₃ recruits Akt, which phosphorylates and opens plasma membrane Ca²⁺ channels
Ras-GTP activates MAPK, which phosphorylates Ca²⁺ transporters
Free Gβγ directly opens voltage-gated plasma membrane Ca²⁺ channels
questions.explain: The sperm-triggered Ca²⁺ wave in an egg is a classic example of IP₃-mediated Ca²⁺ release from ER stores. PLC generates IP₃ from PIP₂, IP₃ opens ER Ca²⁺ channels, and Ca²⁺ floods the cytosol, propagating as a wave. This is the PLC/Ca²⁺ pathway, not the cAMP/PKA or PI3K/Akt pathways.

questions.q: Which description correctly contrasts enzyme-coupled receptors with GPCRs?
options: Enzyme-coupled receptors have seven transmembrane helices; GPCRs have one
Enzyme-coupled receptors are single-pass transmembrane proteins; GPCRs have seven passes
Enzyme-coupled receptors couple exclusively to Gβγ; GPCRs couple to Gα only
Enzyme-coupled receptors act exclusively in the nucleus; GPCRs act at the plasma membrane
Enzyme-coupled receptors use cAMP as a second messenger; GPCRs use IP₃ only
questions.explain: Enzyme-coupled receptors (including RTKs) are single-pass transmembrane proteins, whereas GPCRs are seven-pass. Mixing up these transmembrane topologies is one of the most frequently tested exam traps in cell signaling.

questions.q: Enzyme-coupled receptors primarily respond to which class of extracellular ligands, and what downstream outcome do they typically regulate?
options: Neurotransmitters that regulate ion channel gating and membrane potential
Steroid hormones that bind intracellular receptors and directly activate gene transcription
Growth factors that regulate cell growth, proliferation, differentiation, and survival via gene expression changes
Second messengers such as cAMP that amplify signals within the same cell
Odorant molecules that activate olfactory neurons via GPCR cascades
questions.explain: Enzyme-coupled receptors respond primarily to growth factors and their activation typically leads to changes in gene expression that regulate proliferation, differentiation, growth, and survival. They are distinct from GPCRs (which handle neurotransmitters and hormones) and steroid receptors (which are intracellular).

questions.q: Which three subtypes correctly represent the major classes of enzyme-coupled receptors?
options: Ion channel receptors, GPCR-coupled receptors, and nuclear receptors
cAMP-linked receptors, cGMP-linked receptors, and IP₃-linked receptors
Tyrosine kinase receptors, serine/threonine kinase receptors, and guanylyl cyclase-linked receptors
Tyrosine kinase receptors, lipid kinase receptors, and phosphatase-linked receptors
MAPK-linked receptors, PI3K-linked receptors, and PKC-linked receptors
questions.explain: The three main subtypes of enzyme-coupled receptors are tyrosine kinase receptors (RTKs), serine/threonine kinase receptors (e.g., TGF-β receptors), and guanylyl cyclase-linked receptors. Knowing all three is important — listing only two is a common exam shortfall.

questions.q: What is the correct sequence of events when an RTK is activated by a dimeric growth factor ligand?
options: Receptor dimerizes → ligand binds → transautophosphorylation → docking site formation
Ligand binds → receptor dimerizes → transautophosphorylation of tyrosines → phosphotyrosine docking sites assembled
Ligand binds → cAMP rises → PKA is activated → tyrosines are phosphorylated
Receptor dimerizes spontaneously → ligand stabilizes the dimer → GDP dissociates from Gα
Ligand binds → PI3-kinase phosphorylates the receptor tail → Akt docks directly on RTK
questions.explain: Ligand binding is the triggering event that causes RTK dimerization; the receptors do not pre-dimerize. Dimerization activates the intracellular kinase domains, which then transautophosphorylate tyrosine residues on each other's cytoplasmic tails, creating docking sites for downstream signaling proteins.

questions.q: Each phosphotyrosine on an activated RTK tail serves as a docking site. Which statement about these docking sites is correct?
options: All phosphotyrosines recruit the same adaptor protein, ensuring signal uniformity
Each phosphotyrosine recruits a different specific signaling protein, enabling multiple parallel pathways
Phosphotyrosines are recognized by SH2-domain proteins only if GDP is present on Ras
Docking sites are formed before dimerization to prime the receptor for activation
Phosphotyrosines recruit only serine kinases that then phosphorylate cytoskeletal proteins
questions.explain: A key feature of RTK signaling is pathway multiplexing: each phosphotyrosine sequence is recognized by a different intracellular signaling protein, allowing one activated RTK to simultaneously activate multiple distinct downstream cascades (e.g., both the Ras/MAPK and PI3K/Akt pathways).

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a correct statement about RTK activation?
options: Ligand binding induces dimerization of two RTK monomers
Dimerization activates the intracellular kinase domain of each receptor
The kinase domain phosphorylates tyrosine residues on the adjacent receptor tail
RTK monomers spontaneously dimerize in the absence of ligand to pre-activate the receptor
Multiple phosphotyrosines on the activated RTK create simultaneous parallel signaling
questions.explain: RTKs do NOT spontaneously dimerize in the absence of ligand; ligand binding is the event that induces dimerization. Constitutive (ligand-independent) dimerization would lead to uncontrolled, cancer-driving signaling — which is why some EGFR mutations that cause constitutive activity are oncogenic.

questions.q: A researcher mutates tyrosine Y3 of an RTK to alanine. They observe that signaling protein X no longer co-immunoprecipitates with the activated receptor. What conclusion is correct?
options: Phospho-Y3 is the specific docking site that recruits signaling protein X
Y3 is required for GDP–GTP exchange on the Gα subunit of the associated G protein
Y3 phosphorylation is needed to activate adenylyl cyclase downstream of the RTK
Alanine at position 3 causes receptor misfolding and prevents all downstream signaling
Phospho-Y3 recruits Gβγ to the RTK cytoplasmic tail to amplify signal
questions.explain: Mutating a tyrosine to alanine prevents phosphorylation at that site. If a specific signaling protein no longer associates with the receptor after this mutation, it means phospho-Y3 was the docking site for that protein. This tyrosine-to-alanine mutagenesis strategy is the standard experimental method for mapping RTK docking sites.

questions.q: A researcher expresses a constitutively active (GTP-locked) form of Ras in cells that lack protein Y. These cells signal normally through the MAPK pathway. What does this result indicate about protein Y?
options: Protein Y acts downstream of Ras in the MAPK cascade
Protein Y acts upstream of Ras, because bypassing Ras rescues signaling without Y
Protein Y is the same as Ras-GEF (SOS) and is required for RTK signaling only
Protein Y acts in a completely separate pathway unrelated to Ras or MAPK
Protein Y is a tumor suppressor that inhibits Ras-GTP loading
questions.explain: If constitutively active Ras rescues MAPK signaling in cells lacking protein Y, then Y must act upstream of Ras — because bypassing the Ras-activation step is sufficient to restore the pathway. If Y were downstream of Ras, constitutively active Ras would not rescue the defect.

questions.q: What is the role of Grb2 in the RTK → Ras signaling cascade?
options: Grb2 acts as a Ras-GEF and directly promotes GDP-to-GTP exchange on Ras
Grb2 acts as an adaptor protein: it docks on a phosphotyrosine of the RTK and recruits SOS (Ras-GEF)
Grb2 directly activates adenylyl cyclase to produce cAMP downstream of RTK
Grb2 is a GTPase-activating protein (GAP) that turns off Ras after signal transmission
Grb2 phosphorylates Raf to initiate the MAP kinase cascade
questions.explain: Grb2 is an adaptor protein that lacks catalytic activity. It docks on an RTK phosphotyrosine via its SH2 domain and simultaneously recruits SOS (the Ras-GEF) via its SH3 domain. SOS is the molecule that actually promotes nucleotide exchange on Ras — Grb2 just bridges RTK to SOS.

questions.q: Which correctly describes the function of a Ras-GEF (guanine nucleotide exchange factor) such as SOS?
options: It phosphorylates Ras on serine residues to activate it
It hydrolyzes GTP on Ras to inactivate it and terminate signaling
It promotes dissociation of GDP from Ras and loading of GTP, thereby activating Ras
It anchors Ras to the plasma membrane by adding a lipid modification
It ubiquitinates Ras to target it for proteasomal degradation after signaling
questions.explain: GEF stands for guanine nucleotide exchange factor — it catalyzes the exchange of GDP for GTP on Ras, activating it. This is not phosphorylation (a common wrong answer). GTP hydrolysis is the inactivating step, carried out by Ras's own intrinsic GTPase activity (analogous to Gα).

questions.q: Approximately what percentage of human cancers contain activating mutations in Ras?
options: ~5% of human cancers
~10% of human cancers
~20% of human cancers
~30% of human cancers
~50% of human cancers
questions.explain: Approximately 30% of human cancers harbor activating mutations in Ras that lock it in a constitutively GTP-bound state, continuously driving proliferation without upstream signals. This same 30% figure also applies to drugs acting through GPCRs, but the Ras cancer statistic is a distinct exam-relevant fact.

questions.q: In the RTK → MAP kinase cascade, what is the correct order of signal transducers downstream of active Ras?
options: Ras → MEK → Raf → MAPK → nucleus
Ras → Raf → MEK → MAPK → nucleus
Ras → MAPK → MEK → Raf → nucleus
Ras → PI3K → PDK1 → Akt → nucleus
Ras → adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA → nucleus
questions.explain: The MAP kinase cascade downstream of Ras proceeds: Ras → Raf (a kinase) → MEK (a kinase) → MAPK → nucleus, where it changes gene expression. The PI3K/Akt branch is a separate RTK downstream pathway and should not be conflated with the Ras/MAPK cascade.

questions.q: What molecule does PI3-kinase directly phosphorylate in the RTK → PI3K → Akt pathway?
options: The Akt protein, activating it directly by phosphorylating its activation loop
PDK1, which then recruits Akt to the plasma membrane
An inositol phospholipid (PIP₂) in the inner membrane leaflet, generating PIP₃
The cytoplasmic tail of the RTK, creating additional docking sites
Ras, converting it from its GDP-bound to GTP-bound active state
questions.explain: PI3-kinase is a lipid kinase — it phosphorylates the 3-position of the inositol ring on PIP₂ to generate PIP₃. PIP₃ is a lipid second messenger that serves as a membrane docking site for PDK1 and Akt. PI3-kinase does not phosphorylate proteins directly.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT correct regarding the PI3K/Akt pathway?
options: PI3-kinase generates PIP₃ by phosphorylating a membrane lipid
PIP₃ recruits both PDK1 and Akt to the inner plasma membrane
PDK1 phosphorylates and activates Akt
PTEN opposes PI3-kinase by dephosphorylating PIP₃ back to PIP₂
Akt directly phosphorylates Ras-GEF to amplify the parallel Ras/MAPK signal
questions.explain: Akt does not phosphorylate Ras-GEF. The PI3K/Akt and Ras/MAPK pathways are parallel branches activated by different phosphotyrosine docking sites on the RTK; Akt promotes cell survival and growth by phosphorylating its own distinct set of targets, not by feeding into the Ras pathway.

questions.q: EGFR is activated by EGF binding. Which two major downstream branches are simultaneously activated by the single activated EGFR dimer?
options: The adenylyl cyclase/cAMP/PKA branch and the PLC/IP₃/Ca²⁺ branch
The Ras/MAPK branch and the PI3K/Akt branch
The guanylyl cyclase/cGMP branch and the serine/threonine kinase branch
The Wnt/β-catenin branch and the Notch/NICD branch
The JAK/STAT branch and the Smad/TGF-β branch
questions.explain: EGFR simultaneously activates both the Ras/MAPK pathway (via Grb2–SOS docking on one phosphotyrosine) and the PI3K/Akt pathway (via PI3K docking on a different phosphotyrosine). This multiplexing from a single receptor is a hallmark of RTK signaling, not a feature of GPCR signaling.

questions.q: A patient's tumor cells express EGFRvIII, a mutant EGFR lacking the extracellular domain. Which statement about EGFRvIII is correct?
options: EGFRvIII requires higher-than-normal EGF concentrations to be activated
EGFRvIII cannot dimerize and therefore has reduced signaling activity
EGFRvIII is constitutively active because it lacks the extracellular ligand-binding domain
EGFRvIII activates only the PI3K/Akt pathway because the Ras-docking site is deleted
EGFRvIII is sensitive to anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies that target the extracellular domain
questions.explain: EGFRvIII is a deletion mutant that lacks the extracellular ligand-binding domain. Without this domain, the receptor can no longer be regulated by ligand, and it signals constitutively — driving uncontrolled proliferation. Because EGFRvIII lacks an extracellular domain, anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies (which target the extracellular domain) would not bind or inhibit it.

questions.q: How do EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) mechanistically differ from anti-EGFR monoclonal antibodies?
options: TKIs block the extracellular ligand-binding domain; mAbs block the intracellular kinase domain
TKIs block the intracellular ATP-binding site of the kinase domain; mAbs block the extracellular domain
TKIs degrade EGFR mRNA; mAbs bind EGFR protein and trigger phagocytosis
TKIs activate PTEN to dephosphorylate PIP₃; mAbs block Grb2 from docking on phosphotyrosines
TKIs and mAbs both block the extracellular domain but through different binding epitopes
questions.explain: TKIs are small molecules that compete with ATP for the intracellular kinase domain, preventing autophosphorylation. Anti-EGFR mAbs are large proteins that bind the extracellular domain, blocking EGF binding and/or receptor dimerization. They target different sites on the same receptor molecule.

questions.q: Which acquired resistance mutation arises in EGFR after treatment with first- or second-generation TKIs, and which generation of TKIs was designed to overcome it?
options: C797S mutation arises; 2nd generation TKIs (Afatinib) overcome it
T790M mutation arises; 3rd generation TKIs (e.g., Osimertinib/AZD9291) overcome it
T790M mutation arises; 4th generation TKIs (EAI045) overcome it
EGFRvIII deletion arises; 3rd generation TKIs overcome it
C797S mutation arises; 1st generation TKIs overcome it after dose escalation
questions.explain: First- and second-generation EGFR TKIs (e.g., Gefitinib, Erlotinib, Afatinib) are effective until tumors acquire the T790M resistance mutation. Third-generation TKIs such as Osimertinib (AZD9291) were specifically designed to target T790M-mutant EGFR. Resistance to 3rd-generation TKIs then emerges via the C797S mutation, which is the target of 4th-generation TKIs like EAI045.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the cell division cycle in eukaryotic cells?
options: A repeating sequence of growth, DNA replication, and cell division
A process involving only DNA synthesis and mitosis
A single phase of chromosome condensation and spindle assembly
A series of random events without sequential order
A regulatory mechanism controlled entirely by checkpoint proteins
questions.explain: The cell cycle is defined as growth (G1, G2), DNA synthesis (S), and mitosis/cytokinesis (M), repeating to produce two daughter cells. DNA synthesis alone or random processes misrepresent the coordinated, sequential nature of the cycle.

questions.q: In which phase of the cell cycle does the cell physically increase in size and accumulate proteins?
options: G1 and G2 phases
S phase only
M phase only
Prophase and anaphase
Prometaphase and metaphase
questions.explain: G1 and G2 are growth phases where the cell increases in size and accumulates proteins and lipids. S phase is DNA replication (no growth), and M phase is division, not growth.

questions.q: What is the primary function of S phase in the cell cycle?
options: Duplication of the entire genome
Chromosome condensation
Spindle fiber assembly
Nuclear envelope breakdown
Cytokinesis and cell division
questions.explain: S phase (synthesis phase) is dedicated to DNA replication, duplicating the entire genome so each daughter cell receives a complete genetic copy. Condensation and spindle assembly occur in M phase.

questions.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes G0 phase from G1 phase?
options: G0 represents withdrawal from the cycle and terminal differentiation, while G1 is actively progressing toward S phase
G0 cells are actively progressing toward DNA synthesis, while G1 cells have withdrawn permanently
G0 and G1 are identical phases with different names
G0 cells are dividing, while G1 cells are resting
G0 occurs after cytokinesis, while G1 occurs before mitosis
questions.explain: G0 is cell cycle arrest leading to terminal differentiation; cells do not divide. G1 is an active growth phase where the cell is progressing toward S phase. The exam trap is confusing G0 with G1.

questions.q: What comprises the interphase of the cell cycle?
options: G1, S, and G2 phases
G1 phase only
S phase and M phase
M phase and cytokinesis
Prophase through telophase
questions.explain: Interphase encompasses G1 (growth), S (DNA synthesis), and G2 (growth) phases. During interphase, the cell grows and prepares for division but does not actively divide. M phase occurs after interphase.

questions.q: At the G1/S checkpoint, the cell assesses several conditions before proceeding to S phase. Which of the following is NOT evaluated at this checkpoint?
options: Are centrosomes properly duplicated?
Is all DNA properly replicated?
Are DNA damages repaired?
Is the environment favorable for division?
Has the cell reached adequate size?
questions.explain: The G1/S checkpoint evaluates DNA status, damage, and environmental conditions. Centrosome duplication, while important for mitosis, is not a primary assessment at the G1/S checkpoint; it occurs later during mitosis preparation.

questions.q: What is the primary role of the p53 tumor suppressor protein in response to DNA damage?
options: Directly blocking cyclin-Cdk activity
Activating transcription of the p21 gene
Recruiting repair machinery to damaged DNA
Degrading damaged DNA
Promoting cell division to eliminate damaged cells
questions.explain: p53 is activated by DNA damage and binds to the p21 promoter region, promoting p21 mRNA transcription. p53 does not directly inhibit Cdks; p21, its downstream product, is the actual Cdk inhibitor.

questions.q: How does p21 inhibitor halt the cell cycle during DNA damage response?
options: By degrading cyclin proteins
By complexing with G1/S-Cdk and S-Cdk, blocking their kinase activity
By blocking cyclin-Cdk complex formation through proteasomal degradation
By preventing transcription of checkpoint genes
By accumulating at the kinetochore
questions.explain: p21 complexes with G1/S-Cdk and S-Cdk, directly inhibiting their kinase activity and halting the cell cycle. Unlike cyclins, p21 does not get degraded during this process; its presence itself is inhibitory.

questions.q: In the absence of DNA damage, p53 protein is rapidly degraded in proteasomes and p21 is not produced. What is the consequence for the cell cycle?
options: Cell cycle arrest at G1/S checkpoint
Cyclin-Cdk complexes remain active, allowing S phase progression
Continuous accumulation of p21 protein
Activation of terminal differentiation
Premature entry into M phase
questions.explain: Without DNA damage, unstable p53 is degraded and p21 is not made, so cyclin-Cdk complexes stay active and the cell proceeds through the cycle. If p21 were accumulating or expressed, the cell would arrest instead.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the relationship between cyclins and cyclin-dependent kinases (Cdks)?
options: Cdks are always active regardless of cyclin presence
Cyclins provide regulatory control; Cdks are inactive without their cyclin partners
Both proteins are active only during DNA synthesis
Cdks regulate cyclin expression levels
Cyclins are protein kinases that activate Cdk substrates
questions.explain: Cdks are catalytically inactive on their own and require binding to cyclin partners for activation. Cyclin concentration fluctuates cyclically, not Cdk concentration. This is a key exam trap.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the G1-Cdk complex (Cyclin D + Cdk4/6)?
options: Initiating DNA replication
Driving early G1 progression
Maintaining ongoing DNA replication
Driving mitosis and chromosome segregation
Degrading securin protein
questions.explain: G1-Cdk drives early G1 progression. G1/S-Cdk (Cyclin E + Cdk2) initiates replication, S-Cdk (Cyclin A + Cdk2) maintains replication, and M-Cdk (Cyclin B + Cdk1) drives mitosis.

questions.q: Which cyclin-Cdk pair is responsible for the critical G1/S transition and initiating DNA replication?
options: Cyclin D + Cdk4
Cyclin E + Cdk2
Cyclin A + Cdk2
Cyclin B + Cdk1
Cyclin C + Cdk1
questions.explain: G1/S-Cdk (Cyclin E + Cdk2) controls the G1/S transition and initiates DNA replication. S-Cdk maintains replication once started; G1-Cdk drives early G1; M-Cdk drives mitosis.

questions.q: The M-Cdk complex (Cyclin B + Cdk1) drives M phase and ensures proper chromosome segregation. When is this complex inactivated?
options: After G1 phase ends
During early prophase
When cyclin B is ubiquitylated and degraded by the proteasome
When spindle checkpoints are satisfied
After S phase completion
questions.explain: M-Cdk is inactivated when APC mediates ubiquitylation of cyclin B, targeting it for proteasomal degradation. Without cyclin B, Cdk1 becomes inactive. This degradation occurs after anaphase/telophase.

questions.q: What is the role of APC (anaphase-promoting complex) in regulating the cell cycle?
options: APC prevents DNA replication initiation
APC directly phosphorylates checkpoint proteins
APC marks cyclins with ubiquitin chains for proteasomal degradation, inactivating their Cdk partners
APC synthesizes new cyclin molecules
APC cleaves cohesin rings between sister chromatids
questions.explain: APC is an ubiquitin ligase that tags cyclins with ubiquitin for destruction by proteasomes. This is how cyclin concentration drops cyclically. Separase, not APC directly, cleaves cohesins.

questions.q: Which event is a direct result of cyclin degradation during the cell cycle?
options: Increased synthesis of new Cdk proteins
Acceleration of DNA replication
Immediate inactivation of the associated Cdk
Activation of the APC complex
Immediate entry into the next phase
questions.explain: When a cyclin is degraded via proteasomal destruction, its Cdk partner becomes inactive immediately because the Cdk requires cyclin binding for activity. The cycle progresses as different cyclins rise and fall.

questions.q: During prophase, what are the primary events occurring in preparation for chromosome segregation?
options: Nuclear envelope breakdown and kinetochore attachment
Alignment of chromosomes at the cell equator
Chromosome condensation and centrosome duplication with spindle assembly
Separation of sister chromatids
Reassembly of nuclear envelopes around chromosome sets
questions.explain: Prophase involves chromosome condensation into visible coils and centrosome duplication with mitotic spindle assembly from microtubules. Nuclear envelope breakdown occurs in prometaphase, not prophase.

questions.q: What structure holds sister chromatids together during prophase until they separate in anaphase?
options: Kinetochore proteins
Spindle fibers
Cohesin rings
Centromere sequences
Securin proteins
questions.explain: Sister chromatids are held together along their length by cohesin rings. These must remain intact until anaphase, when separase, activated by APC, cleaves the cohesins.

questions.q: Which statement correctly describes the sequence of events in prometaphase?
options: Chromosomes condense and the nuclear envelope remains intact
Sister chromatids separate and move toward opposite poles
The nuclear envelope breaks down and kinetochores attach to spindle microtubules
Chromosomes align at the cell equator
Cytokinesis divides the cytoplasm
questions.explain: Prometaphase is characterized by nuclear envelope breakdown and attachment of kinetochore proteins (at the centromere) to spindle microtubules. Condensation occurs in prophase; separation occurs in anaphase.

questions.q: What is the primary function of kinetochore proteins during mitosis?
options: Degrading cyclin proteins
Holding sister chromatids together
Assembling the mitotic spindle
Serving as attachment sites for spindle microtubules and pulling chromosomes toward poles
Initiating DNA replication
questions.explain: Kinetochores are protein structures at the centromere region that attach to spindle microtubules and mediate chromosome movement toward spindle poles. Sister chromatids are held by cohesins, not kinetochores.

questions.q: During metaphase, all chromosomes are aligned at the cell equator. What is the critical function of the spindle checkpoint at this stage?
options: To ensure all DNA has been replicated
To activate APC for cyclin degradation
To begin cytokinesis
To verify that all chromosomes are correctly attached and aligned before allowing separation
To repair any DNA damage before mitosis
questions.explain: The spindle checkpoint verifies correct chromosome attachment and alignment before progression to anaphase. This prevents premature separation and ensures faithful chromosome segregation.

questions.q: Which of the following statements distinguishes metaphase from anaphase?
options: In metaphase, cohesins are cleaved; in anaphase, chromosomes align at the equator
In metaphase, the nuclear envelope is intact; in anaphase, it disassembles
Both phases involve chromosome condensation
In metaphase, chromosomes are aligned at the equator; in anaphase, sister chromatids separate and move toward opposite poles
Both phases are preceded by DNA replication
questions.explain: Metaphase is the alignment stage; anaphase is the separation stage. The exam trap is confusing these two distinct phases with opposite chromosome movements.

questions.q: What is the sequence of molecular events that must occur for sister chromatids to separate during anaphase?
options: Cyclin B accumulates; securin is synthesized; separase is activated
Kinetochore microtubules polymerize; cohesin rings condense; centrosomes divide
p53 is activated; p21 is synthesized; Cdk activity is blocked
APC is activated; securin is ubiquitylated and degraded; separase is freed to cleave cohesin rings
S-Cdk phosphorylates cohesin; APC inactivates separase
questions.explain: APC activation is the trigger: it ubiquitylates securin, which is degraded, freeing separase. Separase then cleaves cohesin rings, allowing chromatid separation. The exam trap is forgetting securin degradation.

questions.q: If securin protein is not degraded during anaphase, what will occur?
options: Separase will be immediately activated
APC activity will be enhanced
Cohesin rings will be cleaved prematurely
Separase will remain inactive and sister chromatids will not separate
Cyclin B will accumulate excessively
questions.explain: Securin is an inhibitory protein that keeps separase inactive. If securin is not degraded, it continues to inhibit separase, preventing cohesin cleavage and chromatid separation. This blocks anaphase progression.

questions.q: During telophase, what major events restore the nuclear compartment?
options: Chromosomes condense and the spindle disassembles
Sister chromatids attach to kinetochores
The contractile ring forms at the equator
Chromosomes decondense back to chromatin and a new nuclear envelope reassembles around each chromosome set
Cohesin rings are synthesized and sister chromatids reattach
questions.explain: Telophase reverses the changes of prophase: chromosomes decondense and new nuclear envelopes form around each set of chromosomes, restoring two separate nuclear compartments.

questions.q: Which structure is responsible for dividing the cytoplasm and organelles between daughter cells in animal cells?
options: Spindle fibers
Phragmoplast
Centrosome duplication
Cohesin ring network
Contractile ring composed of actin and myosin filaments
questions.explain: The contractile ring, made of actin and myosin filaments, forms at the cell equator and contracts to divide the cytoplasm in animal cells. Plants use a phragmoplast instead to form the cell wall.

questions.q: How does plant cell cytokinesis differ from animal cell cytokinesis?
options: Plants use a contractile ring; animals use a phragmoplast
Both use centrosomes to organize microtubules
Plants undergo cytokinesis only after mitosis completes
Plant cytokinesis does not divide organelles
Plants form a new cell wall via a phragmoplast; animals form a contractile ring
questions.explain: Plant cells form a phragmoplast (microtubules and vesicles) that assembles a new cell wall in the division plane. Animal cells use a contractile ring instead. Both approaches achieve cytoplasmic division.

questions.q: DNA replication occurs during S phase. What must the cell accomplish before DNA synthesis can begin?
options: Assemble the entire mitotic spindle
Condense chromosomes into visible coils
Align chromosomes at the equator
Degrade securin and activate separase
Synthesize proteins and lipids; grow organelles to be distributed to both daughter cells
questions.explain: Pre-replication events in G1 include synthesis of proteins and lipids, and growth/duplication of organelles. This ensures both daughter cells receive adequate cellular machinery after division.

questions.q: What is a critical consequence if a cell undergoes only S phase and M phase with no intervening G1 and G2 growth phases?
options: DNA replication will not occur properly
The cell cycle will arrest at the spindle checkpoint
Cyclin-Cdk complexes will become overactive
Chromosome segregation will be impaired
Cells will progressively decrease in size with each division
questions.explain: G1 and G2 are growth phases where the cell increases in size and accumulates proteins. Without growth periods, cells divide repeatedly without increasing in size, leading to progressive size reduction.

questions.q: What is the primary function of S-Cdk (Cyclin A + Cdk2) during S phase?
options: Initiating DNA replication and the G1/S transition
Promoting the G2/M transition
Degrading cyclin proteins
Assembling the mitotic spindle
Driving and coordinating DNA replication throughout S phase
questions.explain: S-Cdk maintains and coordinates DNA replication during S phase. G1/S-Cdk initiates replication; S-Cdk keeps it ongoing. S-Cdk activity drops when cyclin A is degraded, ending S phase.

questions.q: Why is it critical that DNA replication (S phase) precedes chromosome segregation (M phase) in the cell cycle?
options: To ensure chromosomes are condensed before division
To permit APC activation and cyclin degradation
To enable kinetochore assembly and attachment
To allow cytokinesis to divide cytoplasmic contents
To allow time for checkpoint verification and ensure each daughter cell receives a complete genome
questions.explain: DNA must be replicated before segregation so each daughter cell receives a complete, duplicate copy of the genome. The cyclin-Cdk activity pattern enforces this order by activating G1/S-Cdk and S-Cdk before M-Cdk.

questions.q: What is the primary advantage of sexual reproduction compared to asexual reproduction?
options: Faster reproduction rate
Generation of genetic diversity
Lower energy requirements
Simpler cellular processes
Greater independence from environment
questions.explain: Sexual reproduction generates genetic diversity through unique assortment of maternal and paternal chromosomes and homologous recombination, allowing populations to adapt to changing environments. Asexual reproduction is actually faster and simpler, but produces genetically identical clones with no variation.

questions.q: During Meiosis I, what separates at anaphase?
options: Sister chromatids
Homologous chromosomes
Individual genes
Centromeres
Non-sister chromatids
questions.explain: In Meiosis I, homologous chromosomes separate at anaphase, reducing the chromosome number from 2N to N. Sister chromatids remain attached during Meiosis I and only separate during Meiosis II, which is why students often confuse the two divisions.

questions.q: A diploid germ cell undergoes meiosis. How many haploid gametes are produced?
options: One
Two
Three
Four
Eight
questions.explain: Meiosis consists of two cell divisions that convert one diploid cell into four haploid gametes, each containing one copy of each chromosome. This is a fundamental outcome of the meiotic process.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes a bivalent?
options: A single replicated chromosome
A pair of sister chromatids bound by cohesins
Two homologous duplicated chromosomes synapsed together
Four haploid products of meiosis
A chimeric protein complex
questions.explain: A bivalent is formed when two duplicated homologous chromosomes synapse together and are bound by cohesin proteins. The kinetochores of the sister chromatids function as a unit during Meiosis I, ensuring proper segregation.

questions.q: What is crossing-over?
options: Movement of chromosomes toward spindle poles
Exchange of genetic information between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes
Failure of chromosomes to separate
Replication of DNA in S phase
Degradation of cohesin proteins
questions.explain: Homologous recombination (crossing-over) involves two similar DNA sequences exchanging genetic information between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes, creating novel allele assortments. This is a major source of genetic variation.

questions.q: In what stage of meiosis does crossing-over occur?
options: Prophase of Meiosis II
Metaphase I
Anaphase I
Prophase I
Telophase II
questions.explain: Crossing-over occurs during Prophase I when homologous chromosomes synapse and recombination proteins create double-strand breaks. It does not occur in Meiosis II, which is functionally identical to mitosis.

questions.q: What is a chiasma?
options: A protein that holds sister chromatids together
The visible point where crossing-over occurred between chromatids
The junction between two centromeres
A mutation in chromosome structure
The spindle apparatus attachment site
questions.explain: A chiasma is the visible cytological marker where crossing-over has occurred between homologous chromosomes. Multiple chiasmata can form in a single bivalent, and their presence confirms that recombination has taken place.

questions.q: How many genetically distinct gametes can a human produce through independent assortment alone?
options: Approximately 8.4 million
Trillions
questions.explain: With N = 23 chromosomes, independent random assortment of paternal and maternal chromosomes produces 2^23 = 8,388,608 genetically distinct gametes. This calculation shows the enormous genetic diversity possible from independent assortment alone, before considering crossing-over.

questions.q: What is the difference between recombination and independent assortment as sources of genetic variation?
options: Recombination is faster than independent assortment
Recombination swaps genes on the same chromosome; independent assortment randomly distributes whole chromosomes
Independent assortment only occurs in males
Recombination requires nondisjunction events
Independent assortment is more common in asexual reproduction
questions.explain: Recombination involves local exchanges of genetic material between genes on the same chromosome, while independent assortment randomly inherits whole chromosomes from each parent. Both create variation but through fundamentally different mechanisms.

questions.q: Nondisjunction most commonly results in which of the following?
options: Increased crossing-over rates
Aneuploid gametes with extra or missing chromosome copies
Reduced fertility without aneuploidy
Mutations in single nucleotides
Inversion of chromosome segments
questions.explain: Nondisjunction is the failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis, producing aneuploid gametes with extra or missing copies of a chromosome. Fertilization with these gametes results in trisomic (2N + 1) or monosomic (2N - 1) zygotes.

questions.q: Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) is most commonly caused by which of the following?
options: Paternal nondisjunction in Meiosis II
Maternal nondisjunction during meiosis
Spontaneous point mutation
Crossing-over failure
X-chromosome nondisjunction
questions.explain: Down syndrome results from the child receiving three copies of Chromosome 21 instead of two, usually from maternal nondisjunction during Meiosis I or II. Risk increases significantly in pregnancies of women over 35.

questions.q: When nondisjunction occurs in Meiosis I, how many of the four resulting gametes are aneuploid?
options: One
Two
Three
All four
None (nondisjunction in Meiosis I produces euploid gametes)
questions.explain: Nondisjunction in Meiosis I affects both products of that division, producing two aneuploid gametes (one with two copies, one with zero) and two normal euploid gametes. In contrast, nondisjunction in Meiosis II affects only one product.

questions.q: What does Mendel's first law (law of segregation) state?
options: Dominant traits always appear in offspring
Two alleles for each trait separate during gamete formation and reunite randomly at fertilization
All traits are determined by a single gene
Offspring always receive genes only from the maternal parent
Mutations occur at a constant rate
questions.explain: Mendel's first law states that the two alleles for each trait segregate during meiosis and recombine randomly during fertilization, with offspring inheriting one allele from each parent. This explains segregation ratios in crosses.

questions.q: In a monohybrid cross of Yy × Yy, what is the expected phenotypic ratio in the F2 generation?
questions.explain: A monohybrid cross Yy × Yy produces an F2 ratio of 3 dominant (yellow) to 1 recessive (green), corresponding to a 1:2:1 genotypic ratio (YY:Yy:yy). This is the classic Mendelian monohybrid ratio.

questions.q: What is the key requirement for Mendel's second law (law of independent assortment) to hold true?
options: Both genes must be dominant
The genes must be located on different chromosomes or far apart on the same chromosome
The alleles must show incomplete dominance
All offspring must be heterozygous
Crossing-over must occur in Meiosis II
questions.explain: Independent assortment only applies to genes on different chromosomes or far apart on the same chromosome (where crossing-over separates them frequently). Linked genes violate independent assortment.

questions.q: In a dihybrid cross of YYRR × yyrr followed by F1 × F1 (YyRr × YyRr), what is the expected F2 phenotypic ratio?
questions.explain: The classic dihybrid cross produces a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio (9 yellow-round : 3 yellow-wrinkled : 3 green-round : 1 green-wrinkled) from 16 possible genotypic combinations. This demonstrates independent assortment of two different genes.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the difference between homozygous and heterozygous individuals?
options: Homozygous individuals have more alleles per gene
Homozygous individuals have identical alleles at a locus; heterozygous individuals have different alleles
Heterozygous individuals cannot reproduce
Homozygous individuals only express dominant traits
Heterozygous individuals always show disease phenotypes
questions.explain: Homozygous organisms carry identical alleles at a locus (BB or dd) and breed true for that trait. Heterozygous organisms carry different alleles (Bd) and produce segregation in offspring. Both can be viable and fertile.

questions.q: Which of the following limitations applies to Mendel's laws of inheritance?
options: They do not apply to any eukaryotic organisms
They apply only to nuclear genes with simple dominance and unlinked genes
They apply only to haploid organisms
They cannot explain monohybrid crosses
They require mutations to be present
questions.explain: Mendel's laws apply only to nuclear genes with simple dominance where alleles assort independently. Linked genes on the same chromosome violate independent assortment; incomplete dominance and epistasis modify phenotypic ratios.

questions.q: Forward genetics approach primarily involves which of the following?
options: Examining genes that are already known to cause disease
Introducing random mutations and screening for abnormal phenotypes
Studying only dominant mutations
Analyzing protein sequences only
Comparing organisms from different species
questions.explain: Forward genetics uses mutagenesis followed by phenotypic screening to identify genes controlling specific traits. Temperature-sensitive mutants, for example, help identify genes regulating conditional behaviors or functions.

questions.q: What does a deletion mutation involve?
options: Addition of extra DNA sequence
Reversal of DNA segment orientation
Loss of a DNA segment
Replacement of one nucleotide with another
Duplication of a chromosome region
questions.explain: A deletion is a structural mutation where a segment of DNA is lost, altering gene dosage and gene order. This is distinct from a duplication (extra copy) or inversion (reversed orientation).

questions.q: Sickle-cell anemia is caused by which of the following molecular changes?
options: Insertion of three nucleotides in β-globin
Deletion of the entire β-globin gene
Point mutation changing GTG to GAG in β-globin DNA
Inversion of a chromosome segment
Nondisjunction of Chromosome 11
questions.explain: Sickle-cell anemia results from a point mutation (GTG → GAG) in the β-globin gene, changing a codon for glutamic acid (hydrophilic) to valine (hydrophobic). This causes hemoglobin to polymerize under low oxygen, distorting red blood cell shape.

questions.q: A point mutation is introduced into a gene. Which outcome is LEAST likely?
options: The mutation produces a normal protein
The mutation produces a nonfunctional protein
The mutation causes disease in all individuals carrying it
The mutation results in a silent (synonymous) mutation
The mutation affects protein function
questions.explain: Many mutations show variable penetrance and expressivity depending on genetic background and environment. Not all mutations cause disease in all carriers — some may be silent, some may be buffered by genetic modifiers, and context matters for phenotypic outcome.

questions.q: Sickle-cell heterozygotes (Ss) show resistance to malaria without severe anemia. This demonstrates which concept?
options: Penetrance and expressivity are context-dependent
Recessive alleles are always deleterious
Mutations never provide benefits
Homozygous individuals are always more fit
Dominant alleles mask all recessive effects
questions.explain: Sickle-cell heterozygotes have malaria resistance without severe anemia, showing that the same mutation can have different phenotypic outcomes depending on genetic background and environmental context (exposure to malaria).

questions.q: Which of the following is a practical application of genetics in medicine?
options: Predicting weather patterns
Identifying disease-associated mutations and diagnosing genetic abnormalities
Determining social behaviors only
Studying only extinct organisms
Analyzing mineral composition of rocks
questions.explain: Genetics allows identification of mutations associated with disease, diagnosis of genetic abnormalities via DNA sequencing, detection of pathogenic outbreaks through genome analysis, and development of targeted therapeutics.

questions.q: How does asexual reproduction differ from sexual reproduction in terms of genetic content?
options: Asexual offspring are diploid; sexual offspring are haploid
Asexual offspring are genetically identical clones; sexual offspring are genetically unique
Asexual reproduction produces more variation
Both produce identical offspring
Sexual reproduction produces clones
questions.explain: Asexual reproduction through mitosis produces genetically identical clones with no recombination. Sexual reproduction combines haploid gametes from two parents, creating genetically unique offspring with variation.

questions.q: In environments with frequent pathogenic pressure, which reproductive strategy would be most advantageous?
options: Asexual reproduction, because it produces more offspring faster
Sexual reproduction, because genetic diversity provides better disease resistance
Neither strategy is advantageous
Haploid reproduction only
Clonal reproduction exclusively
questions.explain: In unpredictable or pathogen-rich environments, genetic diversity from sexual reproduction provides selective advantage. Diverse individuals are better able to resist pathogens and survive ecological stress; this is why sexual reproduction persists despite higher energy costs.

questions.q: When sister chromatids separate during meiosis, which phase is this occurring in?
options: Prophase I
Metaphase I
Anaphase II
Prophase II
Telophase I
questions.explain: Sister chromatids separate during Anaphase II, after the first meiotic division has already reduced chromosome number from 2N to N. This separation requires cohesin degradation and is mechanically similar to anaphase in mitosis.

questions.q: What role do cohesins play in meiosis?
options: They create double-strand breaks in DNA
They glue sister chromatids together until Meiosis II
They synthesize new DNA
They transport chromosomes to spindle poles
They repair chromosome damage
questions.explain: Cohesins are protein complexes that bind sister chromatids together throughout Meiosis I. Their degradation in Meiosis II allows sister chromatids to separate properly, which is essential for producing haploid gametes.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the molecular mechanism of crossing-over during meiosis?
options: DNA strands simply swap positions without breaking
Recombination proteins create double-strand breaks, nucleases digest 5' ends, the second strand is captured, and DNA synthesis completes the exchange
Sister chromatids physically move past each other
Entire genes are duplicated and then deleted
Homologous chromosomes melt and reanneal in reversed orientation
questions.explain: The crossing-over mechanism involves a precise multi-step process: recombination proteins create double-strand breaks, nucleases digest the 5' ends, the second DNA strand is captured from the homologous chromosome, and DNA synthesis and ligation complete the exchange. This produces novel allele combinations.

questions.q: A nondisjunction event in Meiosis II produces one gamete with two copies of Chromosome 15 and another with zero copies. If each of these gametes fuses with a normal gamete, which outcome would result?
options: Two normal diploid zygotes
One trisomic zygote and one monosomic zygote
Two aneuploid zygotes with different chromosome numbers
Haploid embryos that cannot develop
Identical twins with the same aneuploidy
questions.explain: Nondisjunction in Meiosis II produces one gamete with N+1 and another with N-1 (for a specific chromosome). Fertilization of the N+1 gamete with a normal N gamete produces a trisomic (2N+1) zygote; the N-1 gamete produces a monosomic (2N-1) zygote. Most monosomies are lethal in humans.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a primary function of the extracellular matrix?
options: Structural support and tissue compartmentalisation
Providing rigidity to bone and teeth
Serving as an anchorage site for cell polarity
Directly producing ATP for cellular energy
Acting as a signal reservoir and co-receptor
questions.explain: The ECM has multiple functions including structural support, rigidity, anchorage, and signal regulation, but it does not produce ATP — that is the role of mitochondria. The distractor is common misconceptions about ECM roles.

questions.q: The basal lamina differs from the basement membrane in that the basement membrane additionally includes:
options: A thickened layer of collagen only
The reticular lamina
Integrin-binding sites only
Proteoglycan aggregates
Plant cell wall components
questions.explain: By definition, the basement membrane = basal lamina + reticular lamina. The reticular lamina lies directly beneath the basal lamina. This distinction is a common exam trap.

questions.q: Collagen constitutes approximately what percentage of total body protein mass?
questions.explain: Collagen is the most abundant protein in mammals, accounting for approximately 25% of total body protein mass. This makes it quantitatively significant for exam recall.

questions.q: In the collagen synthesis pathway, propeptides are cleaved at what location?
options: Inside the rough endoplasmic reticulum
Inside the Golgi apparatus
In the extracellular space
Inside secretory vesicles
Inside the nucleus
questions.explain: Propeptides are cleaved by collagenase/proteinase in the extracellular space, NOT intracellularly. This spatial control prevents premature fibril assembly inside the cell — a key regulatory mechanism.

questions.q: What is the primary function of propeptide extensions on procollagen molecules?
options: To increase the stability of the triple helix
To enhance secretion through the Golgi
To block premature fibril assembly inside the cell
To recruit protease enzymes
To bind to collagen receptors on cell surfaces
questions.explain: Propeptides specifically block premature assembly of collagen molecules into fibrils while procollagen is still inside the cell. Once cleaved extracellularly, the collagen can self-assemble freely.

questions.q: Standard COPII vesicles are normally 60–80 nm in diameter. What problem do they face when transporting procollagen?
options: Procollagen is too hydrophobic for vesicle membranes
Procollagen rods are approximately 300 nm long
Procollagen cannot be phosphorylated by COPII kinases
Procollagen lacks a signal sequence for vesicle recognition
Procollagen aggregates and blocks the ER exit site
questions.explain: Procollagen molecules are approximately 300 nm long, far exceeding standard COPII vesicle dimensions (60–80 nm). This creates a packaging problem requiring enlarged vesicles.

questions.q: KLHL12 facilitates procollagen export by performing which action?
options: Breaking procollagen into smaller peptide fragments
Promoting enlargement of COPII vesicles
Synthesizing additional collagen molecules
Blocking integrin-mediated endocytosis
Recruiting lysosomes to transport vesicles
questions.explain: KLHL12 is an adaptor protein that promotes formation of enlarged COPII vesicles to accommodate the 300 nm procollagen rod. It does not cleave or fragment procollagen.

questions.q: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome can result from a genetic defect affecting which step in collagen metabolism?
options: Transcription of collagen genes
Translation of collagen mRNA
Conversion of procollagen to collagen
Synthesis of glycine residues
Secretion of collagen molecules
questions.explain: A defect in the enzyme that converts procollagen to mature collagen (by cleaving propeptides) leads to abnormal collagen assembly and hyperextensible skin — a form of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.

questions.q: Fibronectin serves as a molecular bridge in the ECM primarily by binding to which two components?
options: Collagen and hyaluronan
Integrins and collagen fibrils
Proteoglycans and cell membranes
GAGs and osteoblasts
Actin filaments and basement lamina
questions.explain: Fibronectin has collagen-binding sites and cell-attachment sites that interact with integrins, directly connecting the collagen fibril network to cells on their surface.

questions.q: Integrins are heterodimeric proteins composed of what subunit pair?
options: Two α subunits
α and β subunits
A catalytic and regulatory subunit
Heavy and light chains
An extracellular and intracellular domain
questions.explain: Integrins are specifically composed of one α subunit and one β subunit that form a functional heterodimer capable of ECM binding and cytoskeletal linkage.

questions.q: The intracellular domain of integrin connects to the actin cytoskeleton via:
options: Direct covalent bonding
Adaptor proteins such as talin and vinculin
Phosphodiesterase-mediated interactions
G-protein coupling
Autophosphorylation events
questions.explain: Integrins lack intrinsic enzymatic activity and instead relay ECM signals through adaptor proteins (talin, vinculin) that link to actin microfilaments and downstream kinase cascades.

questions.q: Integrin signalling is described as bidirectional. Which statement correctly describes this property?
options: Integrins can simultaneously bind ECM and cytoskeletal proteins
ECM binding triggers intracellular signalling AND cytoplasmic signals can change ECM-binding affinity
Integrins oscillate between the cell surface and the nucleus
Integrin subunits exchange monomers in response to stress
Integrin clustering causes propagation signals in both directions along actin filaments
questions.explain: Bidirectional signalling means 'outside-in' (ECM binding activates intracellular cascades) and 'inside-out' (cytoplasmic signals regulate integrin's ECM affinity), allowing cells to respond to and remodel their environment.

questions.q: Compared to normal breast cells, malignant breast tumour cells express approximately how many times more β1-integrin?
questions.explain: Malignant breast tumour cells express approximately 6× more β1-integrin than normal cells, making integrin–ECM interactions a critical factor in cancer cell behaviour.

questions.q: What is the primary observation when malignant breast cells are treated with inhibitory antibodies against β1-integrin?
options: Cells undergo apoptosis
Cells exhibit phenotypic reversion to normal morphology and behaviour
Cells increase their metastatic potential
Cells lose all surface proteins
Cells differentiate into normal fibroblasts
questions.explain: Inhibitory β1-integrin antibodies cause malignant cells to revert — they resume normal morphology, organise into acini, and stop proliferating abnormally, demonstrating that ECM context regulates cell behaviour.

questions.q: The phenotypic reversion of malignant breast cells by β1-integrin antibodies demonstrates which principle?
options: Cancer cells can be cured only by genetic modification
ECM context regulates cell behaviour independently of genomic mutations
All cancer cells are inherently dependent on collagen
Integrins catalyse tumour suppressor activation
Antibodies can reverse all tumour progression
questions.explain: The reversion occurs WITHOUT genetic correction — the tumour genome is unchanged. This shows that malignant phenotype is reversible by altering ECM signalling, proving context can dominate genotype.

questions.q: Glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) are characterised by which property?
options: They are highly hydrophobic
They are highly negatively charged polysaccharides
They directly bind to cell surface receptors
They catalyse ECM degradation
They are covalently linked to collagen molecules
questions.explain: GAGs are large, highly negatively charged polysaccharides composed of repeating disaccharide units. Their charge makes them strongly hydrophilic.

questions.q: In proteoglycans, GAGs are covalently linked to which molecular component?
options: Collagen fibrils
Integrin subunits
Core proteins
Cellulose microfibrils
Hyaluronan molecules
questions.explain: GAGs are covalently attached to core proteins to form proteoglycans. This distinguishes them from free polysaccharides and determines their functional properties.

questions.q: Which property of proteoglycans makes them well-suited for cartilage function?
options: Their tensile strength
Their enzymatic activity
Their ability to resist compression by attracting water
Their collagen-binding capacity
Their ability to form rigid crystals
questions.explain: Proteoglycans are strongly hydrophilic and attract large volumes of water, making them excellent space fillers that resist compression. Cartilage aggrecan can support hundreds of kg/cm².

questions.q: Hyaluronan is unique among GAGs in that:
options: It is the most negatively charged
It is not covalently linked to protein
It is found only in plant cell walls
It comprises the middle lamella
It is always associated with core proteins
questions.explain: Hyaluronan is a GAG but is NOT covalently attached to protein and is therefore not technically a proteoglycan — this distinction is an important exam trap.

questions.q: In the aggrecan aggregate structure, which molecule serves as the backbone to which link proteins attach multiple core proteins?
options: Collagen
Fibronectin
Hyaluronan
Elastin
Laminin
questions.explain: Hyaluronan backbone — multiple core proteins (with attached chondroitin sulfate chains) link to the hyaluronan backbone via link proteins, creating a large aggregate structure.

questions.q: Cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) contribute to tumour progression primarily by:
options: Destroying the basement membrane
Promoting growth and invasion of tumour cells
Synthesising tumour suppressor proteins
Recruiting apoptotic neutrophils
Preventing blood vessel formation
questions.explain: CAFs are generated/activated during cancer progression and actively promote tumour cell growth and invasion, contributing to the hostile microenvironment that supports metastasis.

questions.q: Lysyl oxidase (LOX) in the tumour microenvironment performs which function related to ECM remodelling?
options: It breaks down collagen fibrils
It cleaves propeptides from procollagen
It cross-links ECM and stiffens the matrix
It synthesises new GAG chains
It recruits immune cells to the tumour
questions.explain: Lysyl oxidase catalyses cross-linking of ECM components, which stiffens the matrix and promotes tumour cell invasion and metastasis — a key step in ECM-driven cancer progression.

questions.q: Matrigel/EHS matrix is derived from which source and used experimentally to restore what property?
options: From plant cell walls; used to test herbicide resistance
From bacterial biofilms; used to study infection
From EHS mouse sarcoma cells; used to restore normal mammary epithelial morphology
From bone; used to increase osteoblast differentiation
From adipose tissue; used to promote lipogenesis
questions.explain: Matrigel is a reconstituted basement membrane derived from EHS mouse sarcoma cells that can restore normal morphology and milk-protein expression in mammary epithelial cultures.

questions.q: The plant cell wall is composed of three major polysaccharide types. Which of the following is correctly paired with its function?
options: Cellulose — fills the middle lamella
Pectin — provides tensile strength against tension
Cellulose microfibrils — cement adjacent cell walls together
Cross-linking polysaccharides — resist compression
Pectin — arranged in crystalline bundles for strength
questions.explain: Cellulose microfibrils provide tensile strength, pectin cements cell walls and fills the middle lamella, and cross-linking polysaccharides fill spaces between cellulose fibrils.

questions.q: The middle lamella of plant cells is distinguished by which characteristic?
options: It is composed primarily of cellulose
It is rich in pectin and cements adjacent cell walls together
It contains collagen-like proteins
It is inside the plasma membrane
It is the primary site of photosynthesis
questions.explain: The middle lamella is the outermost shared layer between adjacent plant cells, rich in pectin, and serves as a cement holding cells together — not part of one cell's wall alone.

questions.q: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between the components of the plant cell wall?
options: Middle lamella → Secondary cell wall → Plasma membrane → Cytosol
Primary cell wall → Middle lamella → Plasma membrane → Cytosol
Middle lamella → Primary cell wall (cellulose + cross-linking polysaccharide + pectin) → Plasma membrane → Cytosol
Plasma membrane → Primary cell wall → Middle lamella → Secondary cell wall
Cytosol → Pectin layer → Cellulose layer → Middle lamella
questions.explain: The correct layering from outside to inside is: middle lamella (pectin-rich) → primary cell wall (cellulose + cross-linking polysaccharide + pectin) → plasma membrane → cytosol.

questions.q: A defect preventing the removal of propeptides from procollagen would most likely result in:
options: Excessive intracellular collagen fibril formation
Inability to secrete collagen from cells
Hyperextensible skin due to abnormal collagen assembly
Increased collagen cross-linking in bones
Enhanced β1-integrin expression
questions.explain: Without propeptide removal, procollagen cannot assemble properly into functional fibrils, leading to abnormal collagen structure and the hyperextensibility characteristic of certain forms of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.

questions.q: Which tissue relies most heavily on proteoglycan aggregates for its mechanical function?
options: Skin
Bone
Cartilage
Adipose tissue
Nervous tissue
questions.explain: Cartilage matrix is dominated by aggrecan aggregates that resist compression by absorbing water, allowing cartilage to support enormous mechanical loads (hundreds of kg/cm²).

questions.q: The experiment showing that normal breast cells require a basement membrane for milk-protein expression demonstrates that:
options: ECM is merely a scaffold with no regulatory function
Cell gene expression depends entirely on genotype alone
ECM context provides instructive signals that regulate gene expression
Integrin signalling is independent of ECM binding
All cell behaviour is determined by transcription factors
questions.explain: Streuli et al. showed that normal epithelial cells only express milk proteins when cultured on reconstituted basement membrane, proving that ECM context provides instructive regulatory signals beyond simple mechanical support.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a mechanism by which the ECM contributes to cancer metastasis?
options: ECM stiffening by lysyl oxidase
Activation of cancer-associated fibroblasts
Formation of leaky tumour blood vessels
Direct genetic mutation of tumour cells
Recruitment of immune cells to the tumour microenvironment
questions.explain: ECM remodelling does not directly cause genetic mutations in cancer cells. Rather, it promotes metastasis through mechanical stiffening, fibroblast activation, vessel leakiness, and immune recruitment.

lectures.title: Lecture 22: Cell Communities, Tissue Formation, and Apoptosis

questions.q: Which epithelial tissue function involves the absorption of nutrients across the apical surface?
options: Protective barrier against pathogens
Absorption of nutrients from the gut
Secretion of hormones and enzymes
Sensory reception of light stimuli
Mechanical support of underlying tissues
questions.explain: The notes explicitly identify absorption — specifically via microvilli-lined epithelium — as a major function of epithelial tissue. The apical surface is specialised for this role in the gut.

questions.q: In an epithelial cell, the apical face contacts the lumen while the basal face rests on the basal lamina. How is the boundary between apical and basolateral domains maintained?
options: By tight junctions sealing the lateral membrane
By adherens junctions linking actin bundles
By desmosomes anchoring to keratin filaments
By gap junctions forming connexon channels
By hemidesmosomes anchoring to the basal lamina
questions.explain: The notes state explicitly: 'Polarity is maintained by tight junctions sealing the lateral membrane between apical and basolateral domains.' This sealing prevents mixing of apical and basolateral proteins.

questions.q: Which of the following junction types does NOT provide mechanical strength to epithelial sheets?
options: Adherens junctions
Desmosomes
Tight junctions
Hemidesmosomes
Both adherens junctions and desmosomes
questions.explain: The exam trap states: 'Tight junctions do NOT provide mechanical strength — that role belongs to adherens junctions and desmosomes.' Tight junctions seal but do not physically link cytoskeletons.

questions.q: Tight junctions create a paracellular barrier that prevents solute diffusion. Approximately how wide is this barrier?
options: 0.1 µm
0.3 µm
0.5 µm
1.0 µm
1.5 µm
questions.explain: The notes state: 'They create a paracellular barrier ~0.3 µm wide, preventing solute diffusion between apical and basolateral compartments.'

questions.q: Which proteins form the sealing strands of tight junctions?
options: Cadherin and linker proteins
Occludin and claudin
Integrin and linker proteins
Connexin hexamers
Keratin intermediate filaments
questions.explain: The notes explicitly list: 'Tight junctions form sealing strands of occludin and claudin proteins that encircle the apical perimeter of each cell.'

questions.q: Treatment with EDTA (a chelating agent that removes Ca²⁺) would most likely disrupt which junction type?
options: Tight junctions
Adherens junctions
Desmosomes
Hemidesmosomes
Gap junctions
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Adherens junctions require Ca²⁺-dependent cadherin interactions; loss of Ca²⁺ (e.g. EDTA treatment) disassembles them.'

questions.q: Adherens junctions and desmosomes both contain cadherin-family proteins. What is the key difference between them?
options: Adherens junctions link actin; desmosomes link keratin intermediate filaments
Adherens junctions require Ca²⁺; desmosomes do not
Adherens junctions are found in epithelial tissue; desmosomes in muscle
Adherens junctions seal the paracellular space; desmosomes do not
Adherens junctions are stronger than desmosomes
questions.explain: The exam trap states: 'Both adherens junctions and desmosomes use cadherin-family proteins, but they link different cytoskeletal filaments: adherens → actin; desmosomes → keratin intermediate filaments.'

questions.q: Gap junctions allow passage of ions, cAMP, and glucose between adjacent cells. What is the approximate diameter of the connexon channel?
options: 0.3 nm
0.7 nm
1.5 nm
3.0 nm
70 nm
questions.explain: The notes state: 'two connexons from adjacent cells dock to create an open channel ~1.5 nm in diameter. This size excludes macromolecules but passes ions, cAMP, glucose, and amino acids.'

questions.q: In cardiac muscle, gap junctions enable electrical waves to spread synchronously. What is the structural basis for this intercellular coupling?
options: Occludin and claudin sealing strands
Cadherin-mediated adhesion to the basal lamina
Connexon hexamers forming hemi-channels that dock to form open channels
Keratin intermediate filaments anchored to the cell membrane
Integrin linkage to extracellular matrix proteins
questions.explain: The notes explain: 'Gap junctions are assembled from connexons: a hexamer of connexin proteins forms a hemi-channel in one cell membrane; two connexons from adjacent cells dock to create an open channel.' This allows coordinated contraction.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a morphological feature of apoptosis?
options: Membrane blebbing
Chromatin condensation
Nuclear membrane breakdown
