Release of inflammatory cytokines into the extracellular space
Apoptotic body formation
questions.explain: The notes explicitly state: 'Apoptosis does NOT trigger inflammation — apoptotic bodies are neatly packaged and phagocytosed. Necrosis releases cell contents and DOES trigger inflammation.' Release of inflammatory cytokines is not a feature of apoptosis.

questions.q: Why is apoptosis called 'programmed cell death' rather than passive cell degeneration?
options: Because the cell actively contracts its cytoplasm
Because it is an active, genetically controlled process with distinct morphological stages
Because it always involves the nucleus
Because neighbouring cells direct the dying cell through specific signals
Because it occurs at a defined time during development
questions.explain: The notes define apoptosis as 'an active, genetically controlled process distinct from necrosis.' It is not passive; it involves specific genetic programmes and specific morphological stages.

questions.q: Which physiological role of apoptosis involves the elimination of autoreactive lymphocytes?
options: Normal development and tissue sculpting
Immune system homeostasis
Tissue homeostasis
Deletion of radiation-damaged cells
Prevention of excessive cell proliferation
questions.explain: The notes list under physiological roles: 'Immune system homeostasis — elimination of used/autoreactive lymphocytes.' This is distinct from general tissue homeostasis.

questions.q: During C. elegans development, how many cells (out of 1090 total somatic cells) are eliminated by apoptosis?
questions.explain: The notes state: 'all 1090 somatic cell fates are traceable; exactly 131 cells die by apoptosis at defined times and positions.' This was crucial for understanding apoptosis genetically.

questions.q: Caspases are a family of proteases that cleave their substrates after which amino acid residue?
options: Alanine
Aspartate
Glutamate
Serine
Tyrosine
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Caspases are cysteine proteases that cleave substrates after aspartate residues.' This specificity is key to their role as death executioners.

questions.q: What is the role of initiator caspases like Caspase-9 in the apoptotic cascade?
options: To cleave nuclear lamin and cause nuclear envelope breakdown
To cleave CAD and release it for DNA fragmentation
To be activated first and then cleave and activate executioner caspases
To inhibit anti-apoptotic proteins like Bcl-2
To directly cleave histone proteins causing chromatin condensation
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Initiator caspases — e.g. Caspase-9; activated first, cleave and activate executioner caspases.' Executioner caspases directly cleave cellular substrates.

questions.q: Caspase-3 and Caspase-7 are executioner caspases. Which of the following is NOT a direct substrate they cleave?
options: Nuclear lamin
CAD (via ICAD inhibitor cleavage)
Bax and Bak pro-apoptotic proteins
Histone proteins
Cytosolic proteins involved in cell shrinkage
questions.explain: The notes list executioner caspase substrates: nuclear lamin, cytosolic proteins, CAD via ICAD, and histones. Bax/Bak are pro-apoptotic regulators activated upstream, not substrates of executioner caspases.

questions.q: Which cellular compartment is the source of cytochrome c release in the intrinsic apoptotic pathway?
options: Endoplasmic reticulum
Lysosomes
Mitochondrial intermembrane space
Peroxisomes
Nuclear envelope
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Cytochrome c is released from the intermembrane space into the cytosol' as the key step in the intrinsic mitochondrial pathway. The exam trap reinforces: 'The intrinsic pathway is initiated at the mitochondria, not ER, lysosomes, or peroxisomes.'

questions.q: In the intrinsic apoptotic pathway, which proteins act as adaptors to assemble the apoptosome after cytochrome c release?
options: Bax and Bak
Cytochrome c and Apaf-1
Apaf-1 and pro-caspase-9
Caspase-3 and Caspase-7
Bcl-2 and Bcl-xL
questions.explain: The notes explain: 'Apaf-1 is an adaptor protein; in the presence of dATP this triggers a conformational change' and the apoptosome recruits procaspase-9 molecules.

questions.q: What is the overall shape and composition of the apoptosome complex?
options: Pentameric ring-shaped complex
Heptameric wheel-shaped complex with 7 Apaf-1/cytochrome c subunits
Dimeric complex of two procaspase-9 molecules
Octameric barrel-shaped structure
Trimeric complex anchored to the mitochondrial membrane
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Seven Apaf-1/cytochrome c complexes oligomerise into the apoptosome — a heptameric wheel-shaped complex.' This 7-mer structure is a key architectural feature.

questions.q: In the Liu et al. (1996) landmark fractionation experiment, which molecule was essential for activating procaspase-3 in vitro?
options: ATP (adenosine triphosphate)
dATP (deoxyadenosine triphosphate)
GTP (guanosine triphosphate)
Mg²⁺ (magnesium ions)
Cytochrome c alone
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Only the fraction containing cytochrome c plus dATP activated Procaspase-3.' dATP is required alongside cytochrome c and Apaf-1 for apoptosome assembly.

questions.q: Which molecular weight does the notes attribute to Apaf-1 based on the Liu et al. fractionation experiment?
options: ~90 kDa
~110 kDa
~130 kDa
~150 kDa
~180 kDa
questions.explain: The notes state: 'The bound fraction also contained Apaf-1 (分子量 ~130 kDa).' This molecular weight was determined from the phosphocellulose column fractionation.

questions.q: How many tissue types are major components of animal organisms according to the lecture?
options: Two: epithelial and connective
Three: epithelial, connective, and muscle
Four: epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous
Five: epithelial, connective, muscle, nervous, and immune
Six: including also support tissues
questions.explain: The notes list: 'Epithelial tissue — forms sheets, linings, glands; Connective tissue; Muscle tissue — skeletal, cardiac, smooth; Nervous tissue — neurons and glia.' These four are the major animal tissue types.

questions.q: Red blood cells are estimated to have a lifespan of approximately how many days?
options: 30 days
60 days
90 days
120 days
180 days
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Red blood cells — ~90 days' when listing cell lifespans in the tissue renewal section.

questions.q: Which of the following cell types are largely post-mitotic, meaning they do not divide throughout most of adult life?
options: Red blood cells and immune cells
Immune cells and gut epithelial cells
Neurons and skeletal muscle cells
Skin cells and gut epithelial cells
Liver cells and kidney cells
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Neurons and skeletal muscle cells — 15–30 years (largely post-mitotic).' The exam trap reinforces: 'Neurons are largely non-dividing; their numbers are maintained by preventing apoptosis, not by replacement.'

questions.q: Dysregulation of apoptosis can lead to disease. Which disease category results from too little apoptosis?
options: Neurodegenerative diseases and radiation injury
Autoimmune diseases and infection
Cancer and uncontrolled cell proliferation
Diabetes and metabolic disorders
Immune deficiency and AIDS
questions.explain: The notes explicitly state: 'Too little apoptosis — cancer; tumour cells escape normal death signals and accumulate.' This is a fundamental principle of cancer biology.

questions.q: Which disease category results from excessive apoptosis?
options: Cancer and lymphoma
Autoimmune diseases
Neurodegenerative diseases and radiation injury
Infectious diseases
Cardiovascular disease
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Too much apoptosis — neurodegenerative diseases, radiation injury.' Excess cell death in vulnerable tissues like the nervous system leads to tissue degeneration.

questions.q: Which scientist(s) were awarded the Nobel Prize for identifying the genetic pathway controlling apoptosis in C. elegans?
options: Sydney Brenner alone
Robert Horvitz and colleagues
Jennifer Doudna and colleagues
Fire and Mello
Barbara McClintock
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Prof. Robert Horvitz (MIT) used random mutagenesis to identify cells that failed to die, then traced the genetic pathway controlling cell death — work awarded the Nobel Prize.'

questions.q: In the intrinsic apoptotic pathway, Pro-apoptotic proteins Bax and Bak perform which action?
options: Release cytochrome c directly into the cytosol
Activate Apaf-1 through direct proteolytic cleavage
Insert into the outer mitochondrial membrane
Inhibit anti-apoptotic proteins like Bcl-2
Directly activate procaspase-9
questions.explain: The notes state: 'Pro-apoptotic proteins Bax and Bak are activated and insert into the outer mitochondrial membrane.' This insertion allows subsequent cytochrome c release.

questions.q: What is the function of the basal lamina (基底膜) in relation to epithelial tissues?
options: It seals the intercellular space between epithelial cells
It provides mechanical support and serves as a substrate for epithelial cell attachment via hemidesmosomes
It secretes cytokines that regulate epithelial cell differentiation
It contains immune cells that protect the epithelium
It stores nutrients for epithelial cell metabolism
questions.explain: The notes state: 'the basal face rests on basal lamina — a sheet of extracellular matrix.' Hemidesmosomes anchor epithelial cells to the basal lamina, providing structural support.

questions.q: How does the caspase cascade ensure irreversible commitment to apoptotic cell death?
options: By recruiting anti-apoptotic proteins that prevent recovery
Through self-amplification: activated executioner caspases can activate more procaspases
By blocking all ATP production in the cell
By preventing synthesis of survival proteins
By directly destroying the mitochondrial genome
questions.explain: The notes state: 'The cascade is self-amplifying: activated executioner caspases can activate more procaspases, ensuring irreversible commitment to death.' This positive feedback loop prevents cell recovery.

lectures.title: Lecture 23: Stem Cell Biology and Cancer

questions.q: Which type of stem cell is classified as totipotent?
options: Adult hematopoietic stem cells
Embryonic stem cells from the blastocyst ICM
Epidermal stem cells
Liver stem cells
Induced pluripotent stem cells
questions.explain: Embryonic stem cells derived from the inner cell mass (ICM) of the blastocyst are totipotent — able to generate all tissues of the body. Adult stem cells are at most multipotent or unipotent, never totipotent.

questions.q: What is the primary source of embryonic stem cells used in research?
options: The trophoblast layer of the blastocyst
The inner cell mass of the blastocyst
Adult bone marrow tissue
Mature skin fibroblasts
The amnion of the developing embryo
questions.explain: ES cells are isolated from the inner cell mass (ICM) of the blastocyst, not from the trophoblast, which serves as the feeder/support layer. The trophoblast does not give rise to ES cells.

questions.q: Hematopoietic stem cells can differentiate into all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Red blood cells
T lymphocytes
Macrophages
Pancreatic islet cells
Platelets
questions.explain: Hematopoietic stem cells are multipotent and generate blood cells: RBCs, T/B lymphocytes, macrophages, platelets, osteoclasts, etc. Pancreatic islet cells come from endodermal progenitors, not hematopoietic stem cells.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the relationship between a proto-oncogene and an oncogene?
options: Proto-oncogenes are mutated forms of oncogenes
Oncogenes are normal genes; proto-oncogenes cause cancer
A proto-oncogene is a normal growth-promoting gene that becomes an oncogene when mutated
Proto-oncogenes and oncogenes are the same thing
Oncogenes exist only in embryonic stem cells
questions.explain: Proto-oncogenes are normal genes that promote cell growth under controlled conditions. When mutated or activated, they become oncogenes that cause unregulated growth and transformation. This is a gain-of-function mechanism requiring only one mutated allele.

questions.q: How many copies of a tumor suppressor gene must be mutated for loss of function to manifest?
options: Zero — tumor suppressors are always active
One copy
Two copies
Three or more copies
It depends on the specific gene
questions.explain: Tumor suppressor mutations are recessive — both alleles must be mutated before loss-of-function occurs. The first mutation makes a person a susceptible carrier; the second mutation eliminates the braking mechanism, leading to cancer.

questions.q: Epidermal stem cells are located in which layer of the skin?
options: The dead, flattened outer layer
The living epidermis layer
The basal layer on top of the basal lamina
The dermis
The subcutaneous fat layer
questions.explain: Epidermal stem cells are unipotent and located in the basal layer of the epidermis, sitting on top of the basal lamina. They continuously divide to replace dead, flattened keratinocytes that are shed in the natural cell cycle.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a key characteristic of cancer cells?
options: Reduced dependence on external growth signals
Indefinite proliferation
Genetic instability with high mutation rates
Complete loss of nutrient uptake mechanisms
Abnormal invasiveness that breaches the basal lamina
questions.explain: Cancer cells are actually avid for nutrients and upregulate nutrient uptake pathways. Loss of nutrient uptake would prevent cancer cell survival. All other options are hallmark cancer characteristics.

questions.q: Induced pluripotent stem (iPS) cells are created by introducing which of the following into adult somatic cells?
options: An enucleated oocyte
Key transcription factor genes
Nuclear material from the blastocyst
Embryonic stem cells
Direct nuclear transplantation
questions.explain: iPS cells are created by introducing key transcription regulators into adult fibroblasts from a skin biopsy, which reprograms them to become pluripotent. This differs from nuclear transplantation (SCNT), which requires an enucleated oocyte.

questions.q: What is the primary advantage of iPS cells over traditional embryonic stem cells?
options: iPS cells have greater potency than ES cells
iPS cells do not require embryo destruction and are patient-specific, avoiding immune rejection
iPS cells can generate more cell types than ES cells
iPS cells were discovered before ES cells were understood
iPS cells are easier to transplant into any organ
questions.explain: iPS cells avoid the ethical issue of embryo destruction and are autologous (patient-specific), eliminating immune rejection. Both iPS and ES cells are pluripotent with similar differentiation potential, though ES cells may retain a slight edge in totipotency.

questions.q: A unique feature of red blood cell differentiation is:
options: RBCs retain their nucleus throughout their lifespan
RBCs lose their nucleus during maturation
RBCs duplicate their nucleus
RBCs develop from embryonic stem cells only
RBCs are not derived from hematopoietic stem cells
questions.explain: Red blood cells lose their nucleus during differentiation — a unique feature among blood cells. This is why mature RBCs lack a nucleus and cannot divide. Other blood cells retain their nucleus.

questions.q: In somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT), which cell component is removed from the oocyte?
options: The cytoplasm
The cell membrane
The nucleus
The mitochondria
The ribosomes
questions.explain: SCNT involves removing and discarding the nucleus of an unfertilised oocyte, then replacing it with the nucleus from an adult somatic cell. This reprogramming can produce either personalised ES cells or a cloned organism.

questions.q: What was the key achievement of somatic cell nuclear transfer demonstrated by Dolly the sheep?
options: Proving that adult cell nuclei retain totipotent potential
Creating the first embryonic stem cells
Generating induced pluripotent stem cells
Developing immunotherapy for cancer
Preventing genetic mutations in cloned organisms
questions.explain: Dolly (cloned in 1996) proved that adult somatic cell nuclei retain the capacity to direct complete organism development, demonstrating that the adult nucleus was not irreversibly committed. This supported nuclear reprogramming as a viable approach.

questions.q: How does the metastatic spread of cancer cells occur?
options: Cells directly jump from the original site to distant organs
Cells invade surrounding tissues, enter blood vessels, travel through the bloodstream, and reinvade distant sites
Metastasis is caused by single point mutations only
Cancer cells travel primarily through the lymphatic system only
Metastasis requires new mutations at each organ site
questions.explain: The metastatic sequence involves invasion of surrounding tissues → entry into blood vessels → travel through circulation → reinvasion and growth at distant sites. This has been directly visualised using fluorescence microscopy showing cancer cells in blood vessels.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains the multi-step clonal evolution model of cancer?
options: One mutation causes cancer immediately
Sequential mutations in expanding clones progressively increase growth advantage and confer survival/proliferation/invasion
All cancer-driving mutations occur simultaneously
Mutations are random and cannot be selected
Clonal selection does not occur in cancer development
questions.explain: Multi-step clonal evolution shows that cancer develops through sequential mutations — each new mutation gives a further growth advantage to the clone, enlarging the population at risk of acquiring additional mutations. This explains the gradual accumulation of genetic instability.

questions.q: Carcinomas, sarcomas, and leukemias differ primarily in:
options: Their mutation rates
The cell type of origin
Their response to radiation therapy
Their ability to metastasise
The number of mutations required
questions.explain: Cancer classification is based on the cell type of origin: carcinomas arise from epithelial cells (most common), sarcomas from connective tissue, and leukemias from blood-forming cells. This is the fundamental biological distinction between these cancer types.

questions.q: What role do DNA repair genes play in cancer development?
options: They promote tumor growth
They normally correct replication errors; when they fail, mutations accumulate in cancer-driving genes
They are only active in embryonic stem cells
They prevent cell division entirely
They have no connection to cancer risk
questions.explain: DNA repair genes normally correct base-pair mismatches and replication errors. When DNA repair fails, mutations accumulate in other cancer-driving genes (both oncogenes and tumor suppressors), significantly increasing cancer risk.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a standard cancer treatment modality?
options: Surgery
Chemotherapy
Radiation therapy
Immunotherapy checkpoint blockade
Vitamin supplementation
questions.explain: The three standard cancer treatment modalities are surgery (tumor removal), chemotherapy (drug-based cell destruction), and radiation therapy (high-energy rays). Vitamin supplementation alone is not a standard cancer treatment, though checkpoint blockade immunotherapy is a modern approach.

questions.q: In PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint blockade, which cell type overexpresses PD-L1?
options: Activated T lymphocytes
Tumor cells
Dendritic cells
B lymphocytes
Macrophages
questions.explain: Tumor cells overexpress PD-L1 (the ligand), which binds to PD-1 on T cells and suppresses T-cell activity — allowing the tumor to evade immune surveillance. Anti-PD-L1 antibodies block this interaction and restore cytotoxic T-cell killing.

questions.q: What is the fundamental difference between oncogene mutations and tumor suppressor mutations in terms of dominance?
options: Both require two mutated alleles
Oncogene mutations are dominant; tumor suppressor mutations are recessive
Both are recessive
Tumor suppressor mutations are dominant; oncogene mutations are recessive
Dominance is irrelevant to cancer genetics
questions.explain: Oncogene mutations are dominant gain-of-function (one mutated allele is sufficient) because they actively promote growth. Tumor suppressor mutations are recessive loss-of-function (both alleles must be lost) because they normally brake cell division.

questions.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes between multipotent and unipotent adult stem cells?
options: Multipotent and unipotent stem cells have identical differentiation capacity
Multipotent cells generate several tissue types; unipotent cells generate only one tissue type
Unipotent cells are more primitive than multipotent cells
Multipotent cells are found only in the embryo
Unipotent stem cells do not undergo self-renewal
questions.explain: Multipotent adult stem cells (e.g., hematopoietic) generate several tissue types, while unipotent stem cells (e.g., epidermal, liver) generate only one tissue type. Both undergo self-renewal; the difference is in their differentiation range.

questions.q: Why would a therapeutic application using transplanted embryonic stem cell-derived tissues face immune rejection, and how could this be overcome?
options: Immune rejection occurs because ES cells are foreign; it could be overcome by using iPS cells from the patient
Immune rejection does not occur with ES cell-derived tissues
Immune rejection is caused by the surgery itself, not the transplant
Only tumor cells trigger immune rejection
ES cells are naturally immunosuppressive
questions.explain: The immune system recognises foreign cells and eliminates them, so ES cell-derived transplants (from others) may be rejected. Patient-specific iPS cells avoid this problem because they are autologous (genetically identical to the recipient). SCNT-derived personalised ES cells have the same advantage.

questions.q: A patient develops a cancer requiring both chemotherapy and immunotherapy. Which statement explains the rationale for combining these approaches?
options: Chemotherapy and immunotherapy target the same cancer mechanism
Chemotherapy reduces tumor size and genetic heterogeneity; immunotherapy restores cytotoxic T-cell killing through checkpoint blockade
Only one treatment is needed; combining them is wasteful
Immunotherapy is only effective in early-stage cancer
Chemotherapy prevents all immune responses
questions.explain: Combination therapy works synergistically: chemotherapy weakens and reduces the tumor burden, while immunotherapy removes the immune checkpoint suppression (PD-L1 blocking), allowing restored T-cell-mediated killing of remaining cancer cells.

questions.q: Why are hematopoietic stem cell transplants effective for treating leukemia, but NOT for treating carcinomas?
options: Hematopoietic stem cells only grow in blood tissue
Leukemia arises from hematopoietic cells, so replacing the blood cell-forming population eliminates the malignant clone
Carcinomas are more aggressive than leukemias
Hematopoietic stem cells cannot be harvested from patients
Carcinomas do not respond to any transplant therapy
questions.explain: Leukemia is a cancer of blood-forming cells, so hematopoietic stem cell transplants replace the entire malignant population. Carcinomas arise from epithelial tissue; hematopoietic transplants do not address epithelial cancer.

questions.q: A laboratory discovers a somatic mutation in an oncogene in one epithelial cell. Which sequence of events best predicts cancer development?
options: The cell immediately becomes cancerous and forms a tumor
The mutation gives the cell a growth advantage → clone expands → additional mutations → further advantages → multi-step progression to cancer
The cell dies because mutations are always lethal
One mutation in an oncogene is sufficient for full-blown cancer
No cancer develops because the cell has other tumor suppressors
questions.explain: Cancer develops through multi-step progression. The initial oncogene mutation gives a growth advantage, expanding the clone. Successive mutations in this and other genes (including tumor suppressors) accumulate, progressively increasing the cancer phenotype.

questions.q: Which therapeutic approach directly removes the immunosuppression mechanism used by tumor cells?
options: Surgery
Chemotherapy
Anti-PD-L1 antibodies
Radiation therapy
Vitamin C supplementation
questions.explain: Tumor cells overexpress PD-L1, which suppresses T cells through PD-1 binding. Anti-PD-L1 antibodies block this interaction, directly interrupting the immunosuppression and restoring cytotoxic T-cell activity against the tumor.

questions.q: A mutation in a DNA repair gene occurs early in a cell's life. Which statement best explains the consequence?
options: The cell immediately becomes cancerous
The mutation rate increases in that cell's lineage, raising the probability of oncogenic and tumor suppressor mutations
DNA repair genes prevent all mutations
The cell becomes unable to divide
Cancer never develops even with accumulated mutations
questions.explain: DNA repair gene mutations allow errors to accumulate uncorrected. This increases the mutation rate in descendant cells, raising the likelihood of acquiring oncogene and tumor suppressor mutations — accelerating the multi-step progression to cancer.

questions.q: Which cancer type would MOST likely originate from pluripotent germ cells?
options: Carcinoma
Sarcoma
Germ cell tumor
Leukemia
Blastoma
questions.explain: Germ cell tumors are derived from pluripotent cells and most often arise in the testicle or ovary. These are distinct from blastomas (embryonic precursor cells), sarcomas (connective tissue), and carcinomas (epithelial cells).

questions.q: How does the potency of hematopoietic stem cells compare to embryonic stem cells?
options: Hematopoietic stem cells are totipotent like ES cells
Hematopoietic stem cells are multipotent whereas ES cells are totipotent
Hematopoietic stem cells are unipotent unlike ES cells
Both have identical potency
Potency is unrelated to cell type
questions.explain: ES cells are totipotent (can generate all body tissues). Hematopoietic stem cells are multipotent — generating several blood cell types (RBCs, lymphocytes, macrophages, platelets, etc.) but not all tissues. This difference reflects their tissue origin and developmental stage.

questions.q: Which statement correctly describes the role of the trophoblast in embryonic development?
options: The trophoblast is the source of embryonic stem cells
The trophoblast is feeder/support tissue; ES cells come from the inner cell mass
The trophoblast generates the fetus
The trophoblast has the same potency as the inner cell mass
The trophoblast is dispensable for development
questions.explain: The blastocyst contains two regions: the inner cell mass (ICM), which develops into the fetus and is the source of ES cells, and the trophoblast, which provides nutritional and developmental support. ES cells do NOT come from the trophoblast.

questions.q: A researcher compares induced pluripotent stem cells to cells created by somatic cell nuclear transfer. Which statement is accurate?
options: Both methods use enucleated oocytes
iPS uses transcription factor introduction; SCNT uses nuclear transplantation — both can produce patient-specific, pluripotent cells
iPS cells cannot differentiate into multiple tissues whereas SCNT cells can
SCNT is the newer technology developed after iPS
Only SCNT avoids immune rejection
questions.explain: iPS cells are created by introducing key transcription regulators into adult fibroblasts; SCNT involves replacing an oocyte's nucleus with a somatic nucleus. Both methods can produce autologous, pluripotent cells that avoid immune rejection and do not require embryo destruction.
